label: Fisher Information
definition: A measure of how much information an observable sample carries about an unknown parameter, equal to the sensitivity of the log-likelihood to that parameter, and serving as the natural metric on a statistical manifold.

body.text: The Fisher information quantifies how much a random observation tells you about an unknown parameter of the distribution that generated it. Concretely it is the variance of the score, the derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to the parameter, and equivalently the expected curvature, the negative expected second derivative of the log-likelihood. Both descriptions capture the same intuition: a parameter is easy to estimate when the likelihood is sharply peaked around its true value, so that small parameter changes produce large, detectable changes in how probable the data is. High Fisher information means a sharp peak and precise estimation; low Fisher information means a flat likelihood and an uncertain estimate. When there are many parameters, the Fisher information generalizes to a matrix whose entries record the sensitivity of the likelihood to each pair of parameters jointly.

Its foundational role in statistics is to set the limit of estimation accuracy. The Cramer-Rao bound states that no unbiased estimator can have variance smaller than the inverse of the Fisher information, making it the currency of how much precision a given amount of data can buy. An experiment with more Fisher information about a quantity is a more informative experiment, and maximum-likelihood estimators are attractive in part because they achieve this bound asymptotically. This is why the Fisher information appears throughout experimental design, statistical efficiency, and the analysis of estimators.

In information geometry the Fisher information is promoted from a scalar quantity to a Riemannian metric, the metric tensor on the manifold of probability distributions. Reading the Fisher matrix as an inner product on the tangent space at each distribution turns the parameter space into a curved Riemannian manifold on which distance measures statistical distinguishability: directions in which the Fisher information is large are stretched, because moving there changes the distribution a lot, while directions in which it is small are compressed. This is the unique metric, up to scale, that is invariant to how the family is parameterized, which is what makes it the canonical geometry of statistics rather than an arbitrary choice.

For machine learning the Fisher information is the object behind natural gradient descent. Ordinary gradient descent moves parameters along the steepest direction in flat parameter space, but that direction depends on the arbitrary parameterization and can move the model's output distribution by wildly different amounts in different directions. Preconditioning the gradient with the inverse Fisher matrix rescales each direction by how much it actually changes the distribution, yielding updates that are parameterization-invariant and far better conditioned. Because the full Fisher matrix is enormous for a large network, practitioners use approximations such as the diagonal, block-diagonal, or Kronecker-factored forms, and the same matrix underlies trust-region policy methods, Laplace approximations of the posterior, and analyses of how loss landscape curvature shapes training. The Fisher information is thus a single idea wearing two hats: a bound on estimation accuracy in statistics, and the metric that gives probability spaces their geometry.
